attributes.stem:
An 85-year-old woman is admitted to hospital following a fall resulting in a tibial plateau fracture. She has a medical history of hypertension, osteoarthritis, chronic kidney disease stage 3, and depression. Her regular medications include perindopril, amlodipine, paracetamol, and sertraline. Her husband of 60 years, who was her primary support person, died four months ago. She has been eating poorly since then.

On examination, she appears frail. Her pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. She has proximal muscle weakness and tenderness over her bones.

Investigations reveal: serum calcium 2.0 mmol/L (2.2-2.6), phosphate 0.7 mmol/L (0.8-1.5), alkaline phosphatase 180 U/L (30-110), parathyroid hormone 15 pmol/L (1.5-7.6), 25-hydroxyvitamin D 15 nmol/L (50-150), albumin 28 g/L (35-50).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management after commencing vitamin D replacement?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with osteomalacia secondary to vitamin D deficiency, likely due to poor nutrition following bereavement. While vitamin D replacement is essential, addressing the underlying social and functional issues is crucial for long-term success.

Comprehensive geriatric assessment is the most appropriate next step as it provides a multidisciplinary approach to assess medical, psychological, functional, and social needs. This will help identify modifiable risk factors for falls, assess cognitive function, screen for depression, evaluate nutritional status, and determine appropriate level of support needed.

Calcium supplementation would be appropriate but should be guided by the results of the comprehensive assessment, which will also address other nutritional deficiencies and determine the patient's ability to comply with supplements.

Bone mineral density scanning would not be helpful at this stage as the results would be affected by osteomalacia. It should be deferred until after vitamin D levels have normalised.

Bisphosphonate therapy would be inappropriate until osteomalacia has been treated and vitamin D levels have normalised. Starting bisphosphonates in the setting of untreated osteomalacia could worsen outcomes.

Physiotherapy assessment will be important but should be part of the comprehensive geriatric assessment rather than occurring in isolation. The patient needs a holistic approach to address all contributing factors to her presentation.

The comprehensive assessment will also help determine the most appropriate discharge destination and support services needed, particularly given the recent loss of her primary caregiver. This may include consideration of home modifications, community services, or alternative living arrangements.
learningPoints: Comprehensive geriatric assessment is essential in elderly patients presenting with falls and fractures to identify modifiable risk factors.
Social support assessment and intervention is crucial for maintaining independence in elderly patients following loss of a spouse.
Osteomalacia requires both vitamin D replacement and addressing underlying social and nutritional factors to prevent recurrence.
references: Cianferotti L. Osteomalacia is not a single disease. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2022 Nov 28;23(23):14896. Available online at ; https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/23/23/14896

Arboleya L, Braña I, Pardo E, Loredo M, Queiro R. Osteomalacia in adults: a practical insight for clinicians. Journal of Clinical Medicine. 2023 Apr 5;12(7):2714. Available online at ; https://www.mdpi.com/2077-0383/12/7/2714
